text.title: Indie Hacker Tax and Legal Essentials Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Indie Hacker Tax and Legal Essentials application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Decide MoR (Paddle, Lemon Squeezy, Stripe Tax/Tax handles VAT) vs direct invoicing (you handle VAT) BEFORE first sale. Choice depends on margin tolerance + ops capacity.
rule.rationale: MoR adds 5-8% fee but offloads VAT + chargeback compliance; direct keeps margin but you handle VAT MOSS, registrations, returns.

rule.statement: Pick entity shape from: home-country sole-trader, home-country LLC, Estonia OÜ via e-Residency, US LLC for non-residents, UK Ltd. Each has tax-treaty + banking implications.
rule.rationale: Wrong entity = repeated structural costs (tax treaty conflicts, banking refusals, double-taxation).

rule.statement: Engage a tax advisor familiar with cross-border digital sales BEFORE first sale. Not after.
rule.rationale: Retroactive cleanup costs 3-10× advisor-up-front; advisor fees are tiny vs penalties.

rule.statement: Document revenue thresholds that trigger registrations: EU VAT (€10k OSS), UK VAT (£90k), US sales-tax nexus (per state, varies), home-country thresholds.
rule.rationale: Triggers fire silently; without monitoring, registrations are missed; penalties accrue.

rule.statement: Banking + payment-processor choice considers data residency + sanctions screening. Wrong bank = frozen account at scale.

rationale.source: Mercury, Wise, Revolut Business, local fintech

rule.rationale: Banks frequently de-risk cross-border SaaS; pick a bank that explicitly supports it (Mercury, Wise, Revolut Business, local fintech).
